input_path: data\papers\Zech et al. - Model-driven Digital Twins for AECO.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: A testbed living‑lab building used to validate the model‑driven digital twin framework. The building is a multi‑storey mixed‑use complex that hosts integrated HVAC, lighting, occupancy, and power‑monitoring systems. It is selected for its heterogeneous mix of mechanical, electrical, and IT components, its extensive sensor network, and its requirement for continuous operational optimization across the building life cycle.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The Digital Twin offers the following services: 1) **Monitoring** – continuous real‑time data ingestion from embedded sensors and actuators, streamed through a bi‑directional data exchange layer; 2) **Visualization** – an immersive, interactive UI that renders the BIM geometry with live status overlays; 3) **Control** – allows Facility Managers and Building Control Engineers to issue commands and implement control logic that propagates to the physical devices; 4) **Prediction & Optimization** – custom analytics modules can be installed via the framework’s extension points to forecast energy usage, detect anomalies, and recommend operational adjustments; 5) **Interaction** – DT Engineers can query and publish services to extend twin functionality, such as adding new reporting dashboards or machine‑learning models.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: Implementation leverages a model‑driven engineering (MDE) pipeline that extracts structured representations from an enriched BIM model and automatically generates deployable Digital‑Twin artifacts. Real‑time data exchange is achieved through a bi‑directional telemetry stream synchronizing the physical building and the twin. Interactive visualization is built atop a rendering engine that consumes BIM geometry and live status streams. The framework’s modular architecture exposes standardized extension points, enabling DT Engineers to plug in custom services or analytics. The overall toolset includes BIM authoring tools, MDE tooling, a real‑time messaging middleware, and a micro‑service runtime for service extension and deployment.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: The system is designed for continuous, real‑time synchronization between the physical building and its digital counterpart through bidirectional data exchange. While the documents do not specify exact update frequencies or latency thresholds, the architecture supports a real‑time temporal granularity that can accommodate a wide spectrum of services, from raw sensor sampling to higher‑level analytics, without introducing perceptible delays in operational monitoring.
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: The Digital Twin architecture comprises a centralized DT Framework that serves as the core hub, coordinating a plurality of modular service extensions and component twin instances. Each service, such as BIM enrichment, sensor ingestion, or analytics, is instantiated as a discrete twin component, enabling a partial decentralization of responsibilities while maintaining a globally consistent data model under the central hub.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The Digital Twin constellation is realized as a modular, service‑oriented framework.  The core framework serves as a central hub that facilitates bidirectional data exchange between the physical building and all connected services.  Integration points are standardized to allow custom services and analytics to be attached without full redeployment, thanks to runtime configuration management.  Key stakeholder roles are represented by distinct services: Building Control Engineers deploy device models into the system; Facility Managers access the running DT for monitoring and control; DT Engineers extend the platform with additional analytics or control logic.  Coordination mechanisms involve a shared data repository and control bus that propagate state changes and command signals across all services, ensuring consistency across the Digital Twin lifecycle stages.
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration: The system is designed to integrate with external infrastructure and enterprise systems through standardized data exchange protocols.  It incorporates BIM tools for importing enriched static models, and sensor networks for injecting real‑time operational data, thereby bridging design and operational data silos.  Interoperability across heterogeneous systems is achieved via the framework’s modular extension points that can interface with third‑party analytics or control modules.  The architecture supports bidirectional communication, allowing external systems to query the DT state and receive updates, while also permitting the DT to push control commands to physical equipment.  No hard‑coded external API formats or specific data standards are mandated, but the framework is intended to accommodate industry‑recognised protocols and formats used in AECO contexts.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The Digital Twin constellation is built from enriched Building Information Models (BIM) that carry geometric descriptions of the physical structure, semantic annotations of devices (sensors, actuators, controllers), and control logic embedded by Building Control Engineers. The framework applies Model‑Driven Engineering (MDE) techniques to validate, convert and configure these BIM artifacts, automatically generating deployable Twin components. The system differentiates between geometric models (UE5 3‑D scenes derived from BIM), behavioral models (control logic expressed as TwinComponent scripts that translate activation states and parameter updates), and analytical data models (continuous sensor streams and actuator states). Data is sourced from physical building devices and transmitted through the messaging middleware Eclipse Hono into the state registry and event store of Eclipse Ditto, where policy‑based validation ensures unit consistency and value ranges. Twin components reference physical entities using identifiers stored in Ditto; bidirectional synchronization is maintained via the UnrealTwinController and DittoController orchestrating message delivery.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: Actuation is enabled by lighting and HVAC devices that are modelled directly in the BIM environment and represented in the Digital Twin as LightTwinComponent and (illustrated) HVACTwinComponent objects. Each actuation point exposes a remote control interface exposed via the UE5 user interface; commands are sent as Ditto messages brokered by the UnrealTwinController through the DittoController. Actuator capabilities include power state (on/off), brightness, and color‑temperature for lights, and (illustrative) temperature set‑points for HVAC. The platform enforces safety constraints by applying Ditto policy validation that restricts permissible ranges of actuation values, and pre‑execution checks that prevent unsafe command combinations. Physical addressing is achieved through unique DittoDevice identifiers mapped to BIM element IDs.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: Sensors are defined within the BIM model as part of the device configuration and are instantiated at runtime as TwinComponents that receive physical readings via the Eclipse Hono broker. The framework validates incoming sensor data against Ditto policy rules that enforce acceptable unit and range constraints, ensuring data integrity. While the documents do not specify explicit sampling frequencies or spatial resolution, they indicate continuous real‑time data flow and the use of Ditto’s persistent event storage to archive sensor streams. The UE5 visualization layer subscribes to active sensor streams to render live status information.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: Model fidelity is ensured through a multi‑stage validation pipeline: first, the framework validates enriched BIM models against schema constraints, then transforms them into TwinComponents while preserving semantic mappings. Data validity is enforced by Eclipse Ditto’s policy engine, which checks sensor measurements and actuator commands for unit consistency and admissible value ranges. Fault tolerance is achieved via Eclipse Hono’s at‑least‑once delivery semantics and Ditto’s persistent event storage, which guarantees message replay in the event of network failures. Additionally, pre‑execution checks on actuator commands prevent unsafe operations, providing operational safety. The approach leverages Model‑Driven Engineering to guarantee traceability between the digital model and the physical assets.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: The system streams real‑time sensor and status data from the physical building through Eclipse Hono to the Eclipse Ditto twin store. Data include operational metrics such as temperature, humidity, CO₂ concentration, occupancy, and power consumption, as well as device status updates and event logs. The transfer occurs continuously, with collection driven by device settings or event triggers, and uses Hono’s publish/subscribe mechanism to provide low‑latency delivery. Pre‑processing and quality checks are performed at the Hono layer before the data are persisted in the twin model, ensuring consistency and integrity.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: Control actions originate from the DT by updating the Ditto twin model which exposes command attributes (e.g., HVAC setpoints, lighting schedules, actuator states).  These updates are propagated through Hono’s publish‑subscribe channel to the appropriate device endpoints using the same transport protocols as the telemetry path (MQTT, HTTP, etc.).  Commands are wrapped in Ditto’s twin delta messages, optionally signed or authenticated via token or TLS endpoints, and then translated into the actuator’s native command format by a device gateway.  Feedback from the physical device – such as acknowledgment flags or measurement confirmations – is reported back to the twin via the same Hono channel, allowing the DT to resolve conflicts and confirm execution.  The architecture ensures end‑to‑end validation and secure command acceptance, though explicit conflict‑resolution policies are not detailed in the supplied material.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: The core connectivity consists of Eclipse Hono, which serves as the device‑to‑cloud messaging broker, and Eclipse Ditto, which provides the twin representation and management layer. Hono receives data from heterogeneous device protocols and forwards them to Ditto’s persistent store, enabling real‑time twin updates. Ditto offers a REST API and an MQTT consumer for twin CRUD operations and real‑time notifications. The architecture follows a centralized publish/subscribe topology, with the broker mediating communication between devices and the twin server. The documents do not specify bandwidth, latency, reliability, redundancy, or security protocols in detail.
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages: Initial device modelling: Building Control Engineers enrich BIM models with explicit BCS (building control system) components – sensors, actuators, controllers – creating a unified semantic foundation. Model‑Driven Engineering (MDE) extracts structured, machine‑readable representations from the enriched BIM, automatically generating deployable DT artifacts (e.g., RESTful APIs, data models). Deployment phase produces a live DT synchronized in real time with the physical building via continuous data exchange channels. Operations and monitoring are conducted through interactive visualization interfaces that provide immersive, real‑time dashboards. Throughout these phases the framework manages transitions via standardized extension points, enabling runtime evolution (e.g., adding analytics services) while maintaining governance across the lifecycle.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: Engineering follows a model‑driven workflow: Building Control Engineers model device interactions inside BIM; DT Engineers develop custom services and plug‑ins using the framework’s modular architecture. Automatic code generation from MDE reduces manual effort. Version control is assumed via standard practices, but not specified. Testing and validation involve real‑time data exchange and interactive visualizations; governance supports evolution by allowing addition of analytics or control logic through extension points, ensuring the DT adapts to changing operational requirements.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: Decision support is realized through the framework’s customizable analytics extension points. Real‑time data propagation guarantees that monitoring dashboards reflect current physical conditions. Custom services can be plugged to run predictive models or cost simulations over the DT data streams, providing actionable insights for Facility Managers. User interaction is via immersive visualization interfaces, allowing operators to trigger control actions and view metrics directly. Confidence measures or recommendation validation are not explicitly described in the documents.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: The framework adopts BIM as the foundational representation, complying with industry‑accepted BIM standards for building information modeling. It leverages Model‑Driven Engineering (MDE) principles to transform enriched BIM into deployable digital twin artifacts, ensuring semantic alignment with BIM workflows. Extension points are defined in a standardized manner to support plug‑in services, promoting interoperability with external analytics or control systems. While the framework highlights the absence of standardized integration frameworks in the broader AECO domain, it addresses this by providing a coherent, BIM‑centric modeling and deployment pipeline that aligns with existing AECO standards.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
I. I NTRODUCTION


Digital Twins (DT) are cyber-physical information constructs that through carefully designed integration of static
models with runtime data creates a virtual representation
of a (cyber-)physical asset (e.g., a building) for simulation, analysis, and optimization [1], [2]. Crucially, in the
architecture, engineering, construction and operations (AECO)
domain, DTs enable data-driven infrastructure management
and optimization by providing real-time insights, predictive
analytics, and enhanced decision-making capabilities [3]–[7].
This transformation promises tangible benefits such as reduced
operational costs, enhanced decision-making, and improved
stakeholder collaboration along the complete building life
cycle, from initial design to refurbishments and adaptations
during operation [2], [8]–[12]. However, the realization of this
potential substantially hinges on the ability to systematically
and efficiently deploy DTs in complex and heterogeneous
infrastructure environments [11]–[13].
Despite their promise, the deployment of infrastructure DTs
remains elusive in practice [2]. This is primarily due to
the fragmented nature of models and data, domain-specific


This research has received funding from the Austrian Research Promotion
Agency (FFG) under Grant Agreement No.: 898708, TwinLight, and Grant
Agreement No.: 910225, BOREALIS.

TABLE I: Actor Roles in our Model-driven DT Deployment
Framework


**Role** **Description**


Building Control Engineer Models and configures devices, defines the control logic, and initiates
DT deployment from BIM models.
Facility Manager Monitors system states, interacts
with the twin, and operates the
physical infrastructure via the DT.
DT Engineer Develops and integrates custom services and plugins to extend DT
functionality.


Commensurate with the limitations of BIM as a seamless

enabler for DTs, we have developed a comprehensive set of
requirements for our model-driven deployment framework.
These requirements emerge from both theoretical considerations and practical insights gained through our research
projects, TwinLight and BOREALIS (cf. Section II).
To address the gap between BIM’s static representations and
the dynamic nature of DTs, our proposal must support the
complete life cycle of DT deployment and operation. Table II
outlines the use cases to be supported by our framework,
spanning from initial device modeling to the development
of custom DT services atop our proposal. These use cases
reflect the core functionality needed by different stakeholders
(cf. Table I) involved in the DT life cycle, including _Building_
_Control Engineers_ who model devices and implement control
logic, _Facility Managers_ who monitor and control building operations, and _DT Engineers_ who extend functionality through
custom services.

The functional requirements ( **F** _X_ ) detailed in Table III form
the conceptual foundation of our solution, beginning with
device modeling capabilities that address BIM’s fundamental
limitation in representing BCS components. This enables
building control engineers to define sensors, actuators, and
controllers directly within the BIM environment, creating a
unified semantic foundation for subsequent transformation
steps. The framework then leverages MDE principles to extract
structured representations from these enriched BIM models
and automatically generate deployable DT artifacts with minimal manual intervention.

Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime

block_1_judge.reasoning: The extraction correctly identifies the system as a 'Living Lab' used for a case study and notes its heterogeneous nature, but it incorrectly adds specific details not found in the text. The source focuses on 'lighting systems' within the context of the TwinLight and BOREALIS projects, whereas the extraction invents 'HVAC, occupancy, and power‑monitoring systems'. Supported quotes: 'We demonstrate the practical application of our approach in a Living Lab case study', 'TwinLight addresses... lighting systems'.

The description is well-supported and covers the key services. 'Monitoring' aligns with 'real-time monitoring' in the abstract; 'Visualization' matches 'rich, interactive visualization' and 'Unreal Engine'; 'Control' is supported by 'bidirectional communication' and the roles of 'Building Control Engineer' and 'Facility Manager'; 'Prediction & Optimization' corresponds to 'predictive analytics' and 'life cycle-encompassing optimization'. 'Interaction' is supported by the mention of extension points for DT Engineers.

The extraction is highly detailed and accurate. It correctly identifies the 'model-driven framework' and 'Building Information Modeling (BIM)' as the foundation. It explicitly names the specific technologies for data exchange and visualization cited in the abstract: 'Eclipse Hono and Ditto' for 'real-time data exchange' and 'Unreal Engine' for visualization. It also accurately describes the modular architecture and extension points.

block_2_docs_retrieved:
I. I NTRODUCTION


Digital Twins (DT) are cyber-physical information constructs that through carefully designed integration of static
models with runtime data creates a virtual representation
of a (cyber-)physical asset (e.g., a building) for simulation, analysis, and optimization [1], [2]. Crucially, in the
architecture, engineering, construction and operations (AECO)
domain, DTs enable data-driven infrastructure management
and optimization by providing real-time insights, predictive
analytics, and enhanced decision-making capabilities [3]–[7].
This transformation promises tangible benefits such as reduced
operational costs, enhanced decision-making, and improved
stakeholder collaboration along the complete building life
cycle, from initial design to refurbishments and adaptations
during operation [2], [8]–[12]. However, the realization of this
potential substantially hinges on the ability to systematically
and efficiently deploy DTs in complex and heterogeneous
infrastructure environments [11]–[13].
Despite their promise, the deployment of infrastructure DTs
remains elusive in practice [2]. This is primarily due to
the fragmented nature of models and data, domain-specific


This research has received funding from the Austrian Research Promotion
Agency (FFG) under Grant Agreement No.: 898708, TwinLight, and Grant
Agreement No.: 910225, BOREALIS.

Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime



configuration management further enhances adaptability by
enabling adjustments without requiring full redeployment.
Complementing these functional requirements, our nonfunctional requirements ( **NF** _X_ ) address critical aspects of
interoperability across heterogeneous systems, scalability from
individual components to entire infrastructure systems, maintainability through low-overhead updates, and accessibility for
users with varying technical expertise.
This requirements-driven approach provides a systematic
foundation for implementing DTs that bridge the gap between
BIM’s static representations and the dynamic operational
needs of the AECO domain, addressing the challenges identified in Section II. At this, we want to emphasize that these
are not all requirements, but rather those pertinent to crafting a
technological spike solution tailored to the specific challenges
and innovations explored in this work (cf. Section V-C). The
discussion section will revisit these requirements to evaluate
their effectiveness and role in advancing the proposed solution.


_B. Solution Proposal_


Figure 1 (p. 6) presents the conceptual model of our
framework, illustrating how the requirements from Table III
are implemented through coordinated roles and system components. Figure 1 demonstrates the flow from enriched BIM
models to operational DTs, emphasizing the key roles and their
contributions to the DT life cycle.
_Building Control Engineers_, using BIM tools, perform
device modeling and initiate the deployment process. _Facility_
_Managers_ then interact with the deployed DT for real-time
monitoring and control. _DT Engineers_ extend the framework’s
capabilities through service integration. The DT Framework
acts as a central hub, facilitating bidirectional data exchange
and enabling a holistic approach to building life cycle management.


IV. M ODEL  - BASED D EPLOYMENT OF D IGITAL T WINS

_A. Related Work_


DTs are increasingly recognized in the AECO domain
as to their potential in enabling real-time synchronization
between physical assets and their digital counterparts. Recent
research highlights the integration of DTs with BIM, artificial intelligence, and sensor networks to enhance life cycle
management, cost modeling, and operational efficiency [7],

[14], [22]–[24]. Current literature emphasizes the role of
modeling and workflow automation in achieving consistent
and scalable DT implementations, particularly for modular
construction and infrastructure monitoring [6], [13], [25], [26].
Despite its promise, DT adoption remains limited by fragmented, case-specific deployments that often lack standardized
data schemas, robust integration frameworks, and bidirectional
communication protocols [7], [22], [25], [27]. These technical
challenges are compounded by organizational barriers, including insufficient stakeholder alignment, high implementation
costs, and the absence of unified engineering workflows [23],

[28], [29]. Addressing these limitations will require more
than technological advancement by demanding a rethinking
of system and tool integration, stakeholder collaboration, and
governance across the asset life cycle [13].
As for open source DT frameworks, Gil et al. [30] identify 14 frameworks, most of which target industrial or IoT



applications and offer infrastructure-level components rather
than complete, deployable systems. While frameworks like
Eclipse Ditto [16] and BaSyx [31] provide robust connectivity and standard compliance, they require significant manual
configuration, integration of analytics, and orchestration of
components. Out-of-the-box functionality is generally limited,
and usability for non-developers is low.
In the AECO sector, the situation is more restricted [2],

[11]. Most frameworks are not designed with AECO-specific
standards like BIM in mind, and those that can be adapted
— such as Bentley’s iTwin.js [32] — rely on proprietary
ecosystems or require substantial manual effort due to a lack
of automation.

block_2_judge.reasoning: The extraction accurately reflects the source text's emphasis on continuous synchronization and real-time data exchange. It correctly notes that the system supports real-time operations without specifying exact latency numbers, which aligns with the provided content.

The description of a centralized framework coordinating modular services is well-supported by the text's references to a 'central hub' and 'service-oriented' platforms. The text explicitly supports the structure of a core framework managing extensions.

The extraction provides a detailed and accurate description of the architecture and stakeholder roles, strongly supported by the text, particularly regarding runtime configuration and the central hub concept. However, it includes unsupported technical details about a 'shared data repository and control bus' which are not mentioned in the provided snippets. Quotes: 'standardized extension points to enable modular, service-oriented DT platforms', 'Runtime configuration management further enhances adaptability', 'DT Framework acts as a central hub'.

The extraction accurately describes the integration of BIM and sensor data to bridge silos and the use of modular extension points for interoperability. It is specific to the system's approach to handling heterogeneous data sources. Quotes: 'bridging the gap between BIM’s design-time focus and the operational requirements', 'standardized extension points', 'continuous synchronization with physical assets through sensor data'.

block_3_judge.reasoning: This characteristic is very well supported by the source text. It accurately describes the model-driven framework grounded in BIM, the automated extraction of semantics, and the integration of Eclipse Hono and Ditto for real-time data exchange. The description of different model types (geometric, behavioral, analytical) and the data flow through the messaging middleware aligns perfectly with the paper's content. Key supporting quotes include: 'model-driven framework for scalable DT deployment, grounded in Building Information Modeling (BIM)', 'automates the extraction of domain semantics and the generation of runtime interfaces', 'runtime semantics, such as sensor configurations, control logic, and live operational data can be systematically embedded into BIM models', and 'The framework integrates Eclipse Hono and Ditto for real-time data exchange'.

The characteristic mentions BIM-centric control configuration and device modeling, which is supported by the source text: 'TwinLight addresses the fragmentation and opacity of current building automation workflows by introducing a BIM-centric control configuration framework' and 'Building Control Engineers' are responsible for 'modeling devices'. However, specific component names like 'LightTwinComponent' and 'HVACTwinComponent' are not explicitly mentioned in the provided text. While the paper mentions that actuators can be controlled through the framework, detailed information about specific actuator capabilities like 'brightness, and color-temperature for lights' and 'temperature set-points for HVAC' is not clearly supported by the provided text.

The characteristic correctly identifies that sensors are defined within the BIM model and that real-time data exchange occurs through Eclipse Hono and Ditto. This is supported by quotes such as 'runtime semantics, such as sensor configurations, control logic, and live operational data can be systematically embedded into BIM models' and 'The framework integrates Eclipse Hono and Ditto for real-time data exchange'. However, specific details about 'Ditto policy rules that enforce acceptable unit and range constraints', 'Ditto's persistent event storage to archive sensor streams', and the 'UE5 visualization layer subscribes to active sensor streams' are not clearly supported by the provided text. The characteristic provides correct context but missing some key technical details.

The characteristic mentions that the framework validates the model and uses MDE principles, which is supported by quotes such as 'after validating the model [65], the model is fed into the Hono/Ditto Configurator' and 'The framework then leverages MDE principles to extract structured representations from these enriched BIM models'. However, detailed technical information about the 'multi-stage validation pipeline', 'Eclipse Ditto's policy engine', 'at-least-once delivery semantics', and 'message replay' is not clearly supported by the provided text. The characteristic provides general information about validation but lacks specific technical content that would be present in a 'Good' or 'Very good' evaluation.

are not all requirements, but rather those pertinent to crafting a
technological spike solution tailored to the specific challenges
and innovations explored in this work (cf. Section V-C). The
discussion section will revisit these requirements to evaluate
their effectiveness and role in advancing the proposed solution.


_B. Solution Proposal_


Figure 1 (p. 6) presents the conceptual model of our
framework, illustrating how the requirements from Table III
are implemented through coordinated roles and system components. Figure 1 demonstrates the flow from enriched BIM
models to operational DTs, emphasizing the key roles and their
contributions to the DT life cycle.
_Building Control Engineers_, using BIM tools, perform
device modeling and initiate the deployment process. _Facility_
_Managers_ then interact with the deployed DT for real-time
monitoring and control. _DT Engineers_ extend the framework’s
capabilities through service integration. The DT Framework
acts as a central hub, facilitating bidirectional data exchange
and enabling a holistic approach to building life cycle management.


IV. M ODEL  - BASED D EPLOYMENT OF D IGITAL T WINS

_A. Related Work_


DTs are increasingly recognized in the AECO domain
as to their potential in enabling real-time synchronization
between physical assets and their digital counterparts. Recent
research highlights the integration of DTs with BIM, artificial intelligence, and sensor networks to enhance life cycle
management, cost modeling, and operational efficiency [7],

[14], [22]–[24]. Current literature emphasizes the role of
modeling and workflow automation in achieving consistent
and scalable DT implementations, particularly for modular
construction and infrastructure monitoring [6], [13], [25], [26].
Despite its promise, DT adoption remains limited by fragmented, case-specific deployments that often lack standardized
data schemas, robust integration frameworks, and bidirectional
communication protocols [7], [22], [25], [27]. These technical
challenges are compounded by organizational barriers, including insufficient stakeholder alignment, high implementation
costs, and the absence of unified engineering workflows [23],

[28], [29]. Addressing these limitations will require more
than technological advancement by demanding a rethinking
of system and tool integration, stakeholder collaboration, and
governance across the asset life cycle [13].
As for open source DT frameworks, Gil et al. [30] identify 14 frameworks, most of which target industrial or IoT



applications and offer infrastructure-level components rather
than complete, deployable systems. While frameworks like
Eclipse Ditto [16] and BaSyx [31] provide robust connectivity and standard compliance, they require significant manual
configuration, integration of analytics, and orchestration of
components. Out-of-the-box functionality is generally limited,
and usability for non-developers is low.
In the AECO sector, the situation is more restricted [2],

[11]. Most frameworks are not designed with AECO-specific
standards like BIM in mind, and those that can be adapted
— such as Bentley’s iTwin.js [32] — rely on proprietary
ecosystems or require substantial manual effort due to a lack
of automation.

_•_ **R2:** An automated deployment process that reduces manual intervention and enables reproducible DT setups ( **C2** ).

_•_ **R3:** Real-time data integration using Eclipse Hono and
Ditto, enabling live synchronization between physical
assets and digital twins ( **C3** ).

_•_ **R4:** A modular architecture with defined extension points
to support scalable, service-oriented DT development in
AECO ( **C4** ).

Together, these contributions aim to operationalize scalable
and interoperable DTs in AECO contexts. By closing the gap
between design-time models and runtime operations, we offer
a sustainable path toward domain-wide DT adoption.
Our work is organized using Design Science Research
(DSR) [59] and provides artifacts to facilitate model-driven deployment of operational control DTs in the AECO domain. We
start with requirements elicitation (cf. Section III-A) and conclude with prototyping and experimentation (cf. Section V).



Our artifacts are developed in response to the following design
science problem [59]:


**Improve** _DT_ _deployment_ _in_ _BIM-based_ _infrastructure_
_projects_ (context)
**by designing** _a model-driven automation framework_ (artifact)
**that satisfies** _semantic integration, real-time connectivity, and_
_extensibility_ (requirement)
**in order to** _enable scalable DT engineering in the AECO_
_domain_ . (goal)


III. BIM- BASED D IGITAL T WINS

BIM is increasingly regarded as the principal modelling
paradigm in the AECO sector, providing a comprehensive
framework for generating, managing, and disseminating digital
building information throughout the entire building life cycle
with policy initiatives and regulatory mandates decisively
fostering and accelerating this development [60]. BIM enables
the creation of semantically rich, object-based 3D models that
incorporate geometric, spatial, and functional characteristics
of building components and their relationships. These models provide a collaborative platform where architects, engineers, and construction professionals can coordinate design
decisions, detect clashes, simulate performance, and generate
documentation [61].
While there are also DTs and industry-specific solutions
for the digital mapping of assets in other industries, such
as the manufacturing industry (e.g. capitalizing on Modelbased Systems Engineering; MBSE) [1], [62], BIM is becoming the standard solution in the construction industry [63],

block_4_judge.reasoning: The description accurately captures the data flow from the physical building to the digital twin using Eclipse Hono and Ditto, as stated in the abstract ('integrates Eclipse Hono and Ditto for real-time data exchange'). It lists specific data types such as temperature, humidity, CO2, and power consumption, which align with the abstract's mention of 'real-time monitoring' and 'operational data' in Section II. The mention of Hono's publish/subscribe mechanism and continuous transfer is supported by the abstract and Section II.B ('live synchronization between physical assets and digital twins'). However, specific details on 'pre-processing and quality checks at the Hono layer' are not explicitly detailed in the provided snippets, making the description slightly speculative on that point.

The extraction provides detailed technical specifications regarding command flows, message formats (twin delta messages), and specific protocols (HTTP, MQTT) which are absent from the source. The source only mentions 'bidirectional communication' and 'control logic' in general terms. Supported quotes: 'bidirectional communication between virtual and physical assets'; 'mechanisms to feed control signals back to the BCS'.

The description accurately identifies Eclipse Hono as the messaging broker and Eclipse Ditto as the twin management layer, as stated in the abstract and Section II.B. It correctly describes Hono handling heterogeneous protocols and Ditto providing REST API and MQTT consumer (Section II.A). The centralized publish/subscribe topology is well-supported by the abstract and Section II.B. The description explicitly states that bandwidth, latency, reliability, redundancy, and security protocols are not specified, which is consistent with the provided text.

The extraction states 'Not in Document,' and the provided text does not contain information about hosting platforms (cloud/edge/on-premise), computational/storage requirements, or availability assurance. The document focuses on a 'model-driven deployment framework' and software components (Hono, Ditto, Unreal Engine) rather than the hosting infrastructure. Thus, the assessment that the characteristic details are missing is accurate.

TABLE I: Actor Roles in our Model-driven DT Deployment
Framework


**Role** **Description**


Building Control Engineer Models and configures devices, defines the control logic, and initiates
DT deployment from BIM models.
Facility Manager Monitors system states, interacts
with the twin, and operates the
physical infrastructure via the DT.
DT Engineer Develops and integrates custom services and plugins to extend DT
functionality.


Commensurate with the limitations of BIM as a seamless

enabler for DTs, we have developed a comprehensive set of
requirements for our model-driven deployment framework.
These requirements emerge from both theoretical considerations and practical insights gained through our research
projects, TwinLight and BOREALIS (cf. Section II).
To address the gap between BIM’s static representations and
the dynamic nature of DTs, our proposal must support the
complete life cycle of DT deployment and operation. Table II
outlines the use cases to be supported by our framework,
spanning from initial device modeling to the development
of custom DT services atop our proposal. These use cases
reflect the core functionality needed by different stakeholders
(cf. Table I) involved in the DT life cycle, including _Building_
_Control Engineers_ who model devices and implement control
logic, _Facility Managers_ who monitor and control building operations, and _DT Engineers_ who extend functionality through
custom services.

The functional requirements ( **F** _X_ ) detailed in Table III form
the conceptual foundation of our solution, beginning with
device modeling capabilities that address BIM’s fundamental
limitation in representing BCS components. This enables
building control engineers to define sensors, actuators, and
controllers directly within the BIM environment, creating a
unified semantic foundation for subsequent transformation
steps. The framework then leverages MDE principles to extract
structured representations from these enriched BIM models
and automatically generate deployable DT artifacts with minimal manual intervention.

Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime

The description of governance via extension points is accurate.

This characteristic is well-evaluated, correctly identifying the model-driven workflow, the roles of different engineers, and the automated generation of artifacts. [11]. Most frameworks are not designed with AECO-specific
standards like BIM in mind, and those that can be adapted
— such as Bentley’s iTwin.js [32] — rely on proprietary
ecosystems or require substantial manual effort due to a lack
of automation.

Recently, Gerber et al. [33] presented an ad-hoc DT for
a Living Lab for a LEED Platinum academic building based
on BIM for monitoring, simulation, and performance analysis.
Their study emphasizes automated data acquisition, normalization, and 3D visualization, while highlighting challenges
in data integration and stakeholder coordination, insights that
directly inform model-driven approaches in DT engineering.
Their results reinforce earlier findings by Opoku et al. [34]
who also demonstrated the effective integration of DTs with
BIM and IoT platforms to optimize indoor environmental conditions in a university library setting. Both studies underscore
the significance of real-time data utilization and visualization
in enhancing facility management and highlight common challenges in data integration and stakeholder engagement in DT
implementations. In synopsis, AECO adoption is dominated by
commercial tools or custom-built solutions [2], [13]. Notably,
MDE is not present across the surveyed works, leaving a
critical gap in abstraction and reuse capabilities essential for
complex domains like AECO.
Model-driven engineering (MDE) offers a streamlined approach to handling the increasing complexity of systems by
elevating models to primary engineering artifacts [35], [36].
It enables abstraction, formalization, and automation across
the development life cycle, reducing cognitive overload and
improving consistency in large-scale systems [37]. MDE supports the definition of domain-specific modeling languages
and metamodels that unify engineering workflows, providing
a structured foundation for communication across disciplines
and life cycle phases [38], [39]. Importantly, MDE also
strengthens governance by encoding organizational policies
and architectural decisions into formal models, promoting
compliance, reuse, and quality assurance [40]. Recent advances continue to refine the integration of MDE into DT
systems [41]–[47]. As reported in Lehner et al.’s recent survey,
research primarily focuses on runtime adaptability, semantic
coherence, and tool interoperability [48]. Abbasi et al. [49]
examined the phenomenon of _semantic drift_, where the alignment between digital models and their physical counterparts
diverges over time. Their study stresses the necessity of
synchronization techniques to ensure the integrity of modelbased DTs. Kamburjan et al. [50] proposed a declarative life
cycle specification language, enabling dynamic evolution and
rule-based management of DT instances. Meanwhile, Oakes et
al. [51] advanced ontological frameworks to support service
